g.data-narration: GPU programming leverages thousands of simple cores for parallel computations, evolving from graphics rendering to general-purpose computing.
g.text: GPU programming writes code that uses

g.data-narration: CPUs have few complex cores for sequential tasks; GPUs have thousands of simple cores optimized for data-parallel throughput computing.
GPU memory hierarchy includes fast per-thread registers, fast shared memory per block, cached constant memory, and slow global memory.
GPU threads organize hierarchically: individual threads execute code, blocks group threads sharing memory, and grids contain all blocks running a kernel.
A kernel is a GPU function marked with __global__. Threads calculate their index using block and thread IDs to process different data elements.
CUDA provides five memory types: global for all threads, shared for fast block communication, registers for fastest per-thread storage.
Amdahl's Law calculates maximum GPU speedup: speedup equals one divided by the sum of serial portion and parallel portion divided by processor count.
g.text: ⚠ Data transfer time adds to sequential portion - can limit speedup!

g.data-narration: The CUDA workflow involves allocating host and device memory, copying data to GPU, launching kernels, then copying results back.
Remember: GPUs excel at parallel tasks, use thread hierarchy effectively, optimize memory access patterns, and minimize CPU-GPU transfers.
g.text: GPU Programming: Unlock Massive Parallelism for Data-Intensive Applications!
